Hey guys, Wobble Wall Street here. Time for your daily motivation slash skills training. I'm in a hotel room in Detroit doing consulting and got a great topic for today, which is what I kind of covered with this company today, which has to do with your sales cycle. And I want to dig into really what are you doing in terms of when you're going out there, like you're in a business where you're call calling it all, right? Or you're even in person selling. Are you using a one call close, two call close, a three call close? Notice how many touch points are there before you actually go out there and try to close the deal. Now remember, there's a close on every call, no matter what. When you call someone and you say to them, listen, I'm calling from XYZ company with the best of this and I'm reaching out to you for this reason. But all I want to do right now is say some information on my company to let you know exactly what we can do. If you think it back to you down the road, next time we can solve that problem, just make them to their requirements, right? Sound fair enough. They say, yeah, sure. Send me the information. Then you start saying, great, just a couple quick questions so I don't waste your time and if you just so I can best serve you. Then you start what's your email address? What's this? What's the next question? What's the next question to get into rapport, right? Versus that's a two-call. Then you essentially, the close there is on closing them to give you information so that you can then send them out an email and a brochure, E-Brosure, whatever it might be, they're on your website, whatever they go, right? And from there, you would then call them back, let's say a week later and say, hey, you're reaching for the call today is last time we spoke, okay? You said XYZ, well right now I have something that's just right up your, I'm just, again, doesn't matter the languages. But the point is this, two calls are about. The first call is when you're basically saying, hey, you know, my name is so-and-so, I come from this company and we can solve your problem, but I just want to send you some information out right now so there's no pressure and that's the key. See, what was happening with this company today and I want to just slow this down for a second now, is they were trying to use a one-call system, meaning they were calling people up and then trying to kind of wiggle their way into selling you something right now. And the irony is, is that when I asked them, well, how many times are you actually selling someone something on the first call? Like never. I'm like, never, like never. I'm like, and why are you opening up the call trying to say, hey, I have something right now to sell you because all it's doing is it's forcing you to get not interested, get hit with objections. In other words, you would never want to do that unless you actually have a really high lack of closing on the first call. Why you set it up as a sale? If you go out and say, hi, I'm from XYZ company. I'm this amazing product offer you blah, blah, blah, blah. They're not interested. They admit you have some kind of objections. They push back at you versus, hey, I'm calling from XYZ company. We handle this specific test, blah, blah, blah, but I'm not looking to sell you anything right now. All I want to do is send you out some information to tell you about our company and then get back to you down the road to discuss things in more detail once you've seen what we can do. Well, next time I have the perfect situation, does that sound fair enough? And they'll say, yeah, sure, go ahead. One sets you up for this adversary relationship where you're knocking heads with the prospect from the first word like, oh, shit, I'm getting sold to versus I'm not looking to sell you a thing today. I just want to send you out some information and get back to you down the road to discuss things in more detail and then watch what happens. Now once they, once you do that, you take the pressure off. So they're like, yeah, sure, go ahead, send me the information. And you say, great, just a couple of questions so I can best serve you. So I don't waste your time. Now we start asking your intelligence gathering questions and what happens? You get to build rapport. You see, because you took the pressure off in the front end of this initial call and they start now they're saying, yeah, sure, I'll ask you some questions because you're not looking to sell me anything. Now once they start answering your questions, you're like, aha, yep, using your intelligence, your active listening, what happens? You fall into tighter and tighter rapport. So let's say now that as you go through these intelligence gathering questions and they give you their answers, let's say all of a sudden they have a problem right now that you can address. Then you can say, oh my God, wow, that sounds great. Just so happens right now that we have a perfect fit that if you have 60 seconds, I'd like to share the idea of you. You got a minute. I'll say sure because now you've built the rapport. You have the right to speak more. When you go out there, I gangbusters, I'm going to try to sell you from the first words out of your mouth. You have such a low hit ratio and it's like you're using, this is using jiu jitsu. You're trying to hit someone like, I can't, but knock them out cold. But he would blow them, boom, boom. When you go out there like a hard charging salesperson, hey, I'm close to this company with the greatest thing in the world. What radio pain? Like I could beat that rate. They're like, I've got to get the f*** out of here versus, hey, I'm actually specializing in this and that. And all I want to do right now is send you some information to show you exactly what we could do for you down the road. And then next time I have some hits right in your sweet spot, give you a call to discuss it in more detail because that's out there enough. And they're like, yeah, sure, why not go ahead? And you say great. Now, what your best, a couple of questions, why don't waste your time. So what's your best email address? They give you that great. Now in terms of your actual business, then go into ask your qualifying questions. And then once you do that, you use that as an opportunity to start building a massive rapport. Now most of the time that will lead you to then sending them some information, right? You send out the information, you give it some arbitrary purpose on three days, five days, seven, whatever I pay, right? Depending on what's appropriate in your industry. And then call them back and you call them. Say if you were called, we spoke last week. I say yes to information by email. Okay. Does that ring a bell? Not that you receive it. If you say that you receive it, they probably said, ah, not really. You say, is that ring the bell? And they'll say, yeah, yeah, it's a great one. The reason for the call today. And then you go into your pitch and you close them. You do it that way over two calls. You have a massive increase in your closing rate because you've built rapport. And the second you've gotten an email from you, you increase the credibility of your company. You're establishing some basis for the second 10. It's a horse of a different color. And the interesting thing is the people I was training today, they know the straight line, but when it came to applying it, they're just so anxious to use to make a sale. However, you can space this out into multiple calls. Now I don't recommend doing it in more than two calls and never more than three. Okay. Unless it's a very rare situation with a really long sale cycle is multiple decision makers. But for most things, you can do it in two calls. You don't need more than that. But trying to close someone in one call on a, you know, cold, cold leaves, not the easiest thing to do. It's easier if you've created an incoming campaign where someone's clicking on your site, you're getting their email and so forth. And then from there, yes, then you can actually go out there. You have a better shot of going out and try to close on one call. But either way, when that first time they hear your voice, remember, it's so much easier to start off by saying, I'm just looking to say, some information and then get back to it. They'll say, yeah, sure, why not? Pressure comes off. Now you step in, it's not asking questions. Use that as an opportunity to build massive rapport on both a conscious and unconscious level. And then if and only if the opportunity presents itself to make a first time pitch where you can actually get there and close on that one call. Yes, then you can transition into that one call close. And that makes sense to do. But don't feel obligated. That should be probably more of the exception to the rule. So I said, let's say you're going out there and making a couple hundred calls a day. Let's say you end up getting 15 leads. That's what you get. You get 15 leads out of the whole thing. Well, off those 15 leads, maybe you'll get opportunity to pitch one, maybe two, without having to apply pressure. It just makes sense to pitch them an idea on that first call. The rest go through the regular sale cycle of three to five days of waiting and then you call them back with the opportunity. It's a much more elegant way to go about doing things and it is perfectly to all the straight line principles. So don't rush the sale. Remember, whether they buy from you, actually buy from you on that first call, there's still a close. The closes is that you're closing them on allowing you to ask that. Yes, I'm giving you permission to ask questions and then so you're closing them that they should disclose the information. You're also closing them on that. You say, listen, I like to give you a call back and they say, sure, to the end, you follow the grade. I got the information now. Next time I have something that's perfect for you, I'll call you back and discuss it and sound good. They say, yeah, great. And you've closed them now on the right to make a call back. That is a much more elegant way to apply the straight line. Okay. Now, remember, when you're doing that sort of two-call process, you're expected to make a minimum of 200 to 250 calls per day. Don't shortchange them. I'm like, oh, hey, 50 calls, that's not good. 200 calls. I love you all. Let's get to work. That system works like magic. Trust me. Apply it. Take the pressure off. And if the opportunity comes and presents itself, yes, you can close the first call, but send information and do it in two calls. Love you all. Talk again tomorrow. Take care.
